response:
Here's a complete, well structured pytest test suite covering all required scenarios. This follows best practices, uses parametrization for clean maintainable tests, and documents behaviour of the implementation:
```python
import pytest
# Replace import below with actual location of your binary_search function
from your_module import binary_search


class TestBinarySearch:
    """Full test suite for the basic binary search implementation"""

    @pytest.mark.parametrize(
        "arr, target, expected, case_description",
        [
            # ==================================
            # ✅ HAPPY PATH CASES
            # ==================================
            ([1, 3, 5, 7, 9],   5,  2,  "Target found exactly at array middle"),
            ([1, 3, 5, 7, 9],   1,  0,  "Target found at first array index"),
            ([1, 3, 5, 7, 9],   9,  4,  "Target found at last array index"),
            ([2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12], 4, 1, "Target found in left half of array"),
            ([2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12], 10,4, "Target found in right half of array"),
            ([42],              42, 0,  "Single element array, target matches"),
            ([1,1,2,2,3,3],     2,  2,  "Duplicate elements, returns valid matching index"),

            # ==================================
            # ⚠️ EDGE CASES (NOT FOUND)
            # ==================================
            ([],                5, -1,  "Empty input array always returns -1"),
            ([42],              7, -1,  "Single element array, target does not match"),
            ([1, 3, 5, 7],      0, -1,  "Target smaller than all array values"),
            ([1, 3, 5, 7],     10, -1,  "Target larger than all array values"),
            ([2, 4, 6, 8],      5, -1,  "Target missing, lies between existing values"),
        ]
    )
    def test_correct_return_value(self, arr, target, expected, case_description):
        """Test all standard return scenarios for valid input"""
        assert binary_search(arr, target) == expected, f"FAIL: {case_description}"

    # ==================================
    # ❌ ERROR / INVALID INPUT CASES
    # ==================================
    def test_non_list_input_raises_type_error(self):
        """Verify passing non-list value for array argument correctly errors"""
        with pytest.raises(TypeError):
            binary_search("this is not an array", 3)

    def test_non_integer_target_raises_type_error(self):
        """Verify mismatched target type correctly errors"""
        with pytest.raises(TypeError):
            binary_search([1,2,3,4], "string target")

    def test_unsorted_input_behaviour(self):
        """⚠️ Critical documented behaviour: this implementation REQUIRES pre-sorted input.
        It will SILENTLY return incorrect results for unsorted arrays with no error.
        """
        unsorted_arr = [9, 1, 5, 3, 7]
        # Value 1 exists in array, but will not be found
        assert binary_search(unsorted_arr, 1) == -1
```

---
### Coverage Breakdown:
| Category | What is tested |
|---|---|
| ✅ Happy Path | All valid target positions, odd/even length arrays, single element matches, duplicate values |
| ⚠️ Edge Cases | Empty array, out of bounds targets, missing values between existing elements |
| ❌ Error Cases | Invalid input types, plus explicit demonstration of the sorted input requirement (the #1 gotcha for binary search) |

Run tests with verbose output using:
```bash
pytest test_binary_search.py -v
```
Every test case includes a human readable description that will be printed directly on test failure for fast debugging.
